original_abstract:
The rapid proliferation of location-based services (LBS) has enabled untrusted servers to harvest vast amounts of precise user data, sparking significant privacy concerns. In this paper, we introduce and formalize **geo-indistinguishability**, a privacy standard designed specifically for location-based systems. This notion protects a user’s exact coordinates while allowing for the release of the approximate information necessary to receive a desired service.

Our definition formalizes the intuitive requirement that a user's location should be protected within a radius $r$, with a privacy level that scales according to $r$. This approach represents a generalized version of the well-established differential privacy framework. To achieve geo-indistinguishability in practice, we present a perturbation technique that adds controlled random noise to the user’s location. 

We demonstrate the real-world applicability of our method through an LBS case study and compare our mechanism against existing techniques in the literature. Our findings show that among all mechanisms that function independently of an adversary's prior knowledge, ours provides the strongest privacy guarantees for a given level of utility.
humanized_abstract:
With the proliferation of Location-Based Services (LBS) such as Groupon, Foursquare, and other location-aware systems, untrusted servers now have access to very personal information about users such as their current location or route. This has raised serious privacy concerns. This work introduces and formalizes a privacy definition called geo-indistinguishability, tailored for location-based systems, which preserves the privacy of user locations and releases possibly less accurate locations in order to receive a desired service.

Our formalization follows from the intuitive requirement that a user's location should be kept private up to some radius r, say, with privacy level depending on r. This is a more general notion than differential privacy. We give a mechanism to achieve geo-indistinguishability. The idea is to perturb the user's true location by adding some noise.

We evaluate the practicality of the proposed method in a real-world Location-Based Service (LBS) scenario and compare it with other works in the literature. The result confirms that our mechanism provides the highest level of privacy for a given level of utility among all mechanisms that are oblivious to the attackers' a priori knowledge.

document.output:

This has raised serious privacy concerns. This work introduces and formalizes a privacy definition called geo-indistinguishability, tailored for location-based systems, which preserves the privacy of user locations and releases possibly less accurate locations in order to receive a desired service.

Our formalization follows from the intuitive requirement that a user's location should be kept private up to some radius r, say, with privacy level depending on r. This is a more general notion than differential privacy. We give a mechanism to achieve geo-indistinguishability. The idea is to perturb the user's true location by adding some noise.

We evaluate the practicality of the proposed method in a real-world Location-Based Service (LBS) scenario and compare it with other works in the literature. The result confirms that our mechanism provides the highest level of privacy for a given level of utility among all mechanisms that are oblivious to the attackers' a priori knowledge.
document.input:
The rapid proliferation of location-based services (LBS) has enabled untrusted servers to harvest vast amounts of precise user data, sparking significant privacy concerns. In this paper, we introduce and formalize **geo-indistinguishability**, a privacy standard designed specifically for location-based systems. This notion protects a user’s exact coordinates while allowing for the release of the approximate information necessary to receive a desired service.

Our definition formalizes the intuitive requirement that a user's location should be protected within a radius $r$, with a privacy level that scales according to $r$. This approach represents a generalized version of the well-established differential privacy framework. To achieve geo-indistinguishability in practice, we present a perturbation technique that adds controlled random noise to the user’s location. 

We demonstrate the real-world applicability of our method through an LBS case study and compare our mechanism against existing techniques in the literature. Our findings show that among all mechanisms that function independently of an adversary's prior knowledge, ours provides the strongest privacy guarantees for a given level of utility.
